topic: A person finds out their article was published under someone else's name
raw_output:
[story 1]

The email landed in my inbox like a lead weight. My stomach clenched, a cold, tight knot forming beneath my ribs. I clicked it open, my fingers trembling slightly above the keyboard. The headline mocked me: "Groundbreaking Insights from Dr. Evelyn Reed." My name, attached to the byline, was nowhere to be found. I reread the text, the carefully crafted sentences that were undeniably mine, yet they belonged to a stranger. A wave of nausea washed over me, and I leaned back in my worn office chair, the springs groaning in protest. All the late nights, the meticulous research, the sheer effort—reduced to a ghost’s footnote. I stared blankly at the wall, the fluorescent lights buzzing overhead seeming to amplify the hollow echo in my chest.

[story 2]

I sat at the kitchen table, the lukewarm coffee forgotten in my hand. The notification flashed on my screen, and I felt a sudden, sharp drop in my energy. It wasn't anger that surfaced, just a profound emptiness. I slowly pushed my chair back, the scrape of metal against the tile sounding unnervingly loud in the quiet apartment. I picked up the printed proof, the ink stark and unforgiving. The words were there, vibrant and true, but the ownership was stolen. I traced a line of text with a fingertip, the texture of the paper feeling alien beneath my touch. A heavy sigh escaped my lips, a sound laced with a weary resignation. Why bother pouring so much of myself into something that wasn't mine to claim?

[story 3]

The phone call ended, leaving a ringing silence in its wake. I stood by the window, watching the city lights blur into streaks of indifferent color. My hands were cold, clammy against the glass. When I finally opened the document, the realization hit with the dull thud of something heavy falling onto concrete. It was published under a pseudonym. A clever, infuriating maneuver. I walked to the desk, my movements slow and deliberate, as if wading through thick mud. I didn't bother to delete the file or send an immediate reply. Instead, I just sat down heavily, letting the weight of the situation settle. My shoulders slumped forward, a visible surrender to the sheer unfairness of it all.
